kw.name: Append expected results
kw.doc:
Append expected results
argument: - caller: caller function name - callee: callee function name
Examples: | Append expected results | main | initialize |

kw.name: Check timeliness
kw.doc:
Check timeliness - this should be called after compare - check time diff from first expected result to last expected result
argument: - time_diff: milliseconds
return: - True: if last function of expected results called within time_diff milliseconds - False: if not last function of expected results called within time_diff milliseconds
Examples: | Check timeliness | 100 |
kw.shortdoc: Check timeliness - this should be called after compare - check time diff from first expected result to last expected result

kw.name: Compare expected to actual
kw.doc:
Compare expected results to actual results
argument:
return: [ { 'caller': func, 'callee': func, 'result': boolean, 'timestamp': unix timestamp (microseconds) }, { 'caller': func, 'callee': func, 'result': boolean, 'timestamp': unix timestamp (microseconds) } ]
Examples: | Compare expected to actual |
kw.shortdoc: Compare expected results to actual results

kw.name: Get basename of trace file
kw.doc:
Get basename of trace file
argument: - trace_file: trace log file
Examples: | Get basename of trace file | ${LOG PATH}\___lge___app_ro___network___network |

kw.name: Set actual results
kw.doc:
Set actual results - concatenate all parsed files in a path - sorting by time ascending - save to csv file
argument: - path: directory in local pc
Examples: | Set actual results | ${TRACE_LOG_PATH} |
kw.shortdoc: Set actual results - concatenate all parsed files in a path - sorting by time ascending - save to csv file

kw.name: Set start timestamp
kw.doc:
Set trace start timestamp - convert seconds to microseconds - convert string to int
argument: - timestamp: the started seconds since the epoch of target
Examples: | Set start timestamp | timestamp |
kw.shortdoc: Set trace start timestamp - convert seconds to microseconds - convert string to int

type.name: boolean
type.doc:
Strings ``TRUE``, ``YES``, ``ON`` and ``1`` are converted to Boolean ``True``, the empty string as well as strings ``FALSE``, ``NO``, ``OFF`` and ``0`` are converted to Boolean ``False``, and the string ``NONE`` is converted to the Python ``None`` object. Other strings and other accepted values are passed as-is, allowing keywords to handle them specially if needed. All string comparisons are case-insensitive.
Examples: ``TRUE`` (converted to ``True``), ``off`` (converted to ``False``), ``example`` (used as-is)

type.name: integer
type.doc:
Conversion is done using Python's [https://docs.python.org/library/functions.html#int|int] built-in function. Floating point numbers are accepted only if they can be represented as integers exactly. For example, ``1.0`` is accepted and ``1.1`` is not.
Starting from RF 4.1, it is possible to use hexadecimal, octal and binary numbers by prefixing values with ``0x``, ``0o`` and ``0b``, respectively.
Starting from RF 4.1, spaces and underscores can be used as visual separators for digit grouping purposes.
Examples: ``42``, ``-1``, ``0b1010``, ``10 000 000``, ``0xBAD_C0FFEE``

type.name: list
type.doc:
Strings must be Python [https://docs.python.org/library/stdtypes.html#list|list] literals. They are converted to actual lists using the [https://docs.python.org/library/ast.html#ast.literal_eval|ast.literal_eval] function. They can contain any values ``ast.literal_eval`` supports, including lists and other containers.
If the type has nested types like ``list[int]``, items are converted to those types automatically. This in new in Robot Framework 6.0.
Examples: ``['one', 'two']``, ``[('one', 1), ('two', 2)]``

type.name: string
type.doc: All arguments are converted to Unicode strings.
